reason_raw: Tech Reviewed (F) Incorrect Imaging Protocol
comment_raw:
From the Tech-
"
This pt came over with an unusable IV and had him sent back. The second IV was usable but stung a little when flushed to which I told him this was normal but it being painful was not. Started the scan per protocol and contrast at 3.5 and would work up,
bolus tracking started and the pt started yelling, so I paused the contrast and ran out to check the line. Unfortunately, I thought I hit stop on the scanner and did not, so when the machine said stop breathing I was already headed back in and I stoppedthe scanner. (That was our first partial non-con study.) I then restarted the bolus and when I went to scan I did not realize that the scanner would only scan the portion previously scanned and not the FOV chosen at the start. At that point I had to
think fast to catch whatever contrast I could in the lower bases of the lungs without a re-bolus. The third scan is the remaining portion. Before I took the pt off the table I called the rads and asked if they could look at the hodge podge of images. I
was told they could see what they needed without re-bolus and they asked what went wrong, I explained and we moved on". 
It has not be determined which Radiologist the teammate spoke with.


Rad-
This study is a mess with no explanation -it was repeated with half the chest scanned with 2 separate acquisitions.  Wrong FOV.  Please find out what happened here it is really not acceptable.

procedure_raw: CT Endo Chest Abdomen Pelvis W Contrast
procedure_normalized: CT ENDO CHEST ABDOMEN PELVIS W/IV CONTRAST
reason_raw: Tech Reviewed (M) Incorrect bolus timing
comment_raw:
The scanner outran the bolus. Future examinations need to be triggered from the iliac arteries.

If you want something outside of our normal protocol for a patient, I would suggest that it get's protocolled that way. But as a technologist, the expectation should be that we follow the written protocol that is requested. I happened to scan this
patient, and I did not time the bolus incorrectly. Per the protocol, I waited 15 secs before beginning monitoring at the level of the diaphragm. The PIV was a vat team placed line in the mid-forearm, and I injected at the maximum that the PIV could
tolerate (4ml/sec). This is within range of 4-5ml/sec listed on the protocol. Through no fault of my own, it took 14 seconds to trigger at this level. Then there was a 6 second delay as the table moved into place and it gave her the breathing
instructions. Despite the Impression listing that the flash scanner outran the contrast bolus, it was just a normal image acquisition (non-flash), with a scan time of just over 7 seconds.That means that it was about 40 seconds after I began the contrast(and 15 seconds after the contrast was done injecting) that the scanner reached the level of the iliacs. I did see that we started to lose some contrast enhancement looking at the real time data. However, because of the time it takes to inspect your
images for quality and the scanner to do be able to do an unplanned repeat, we cannot usually do a lot when this type of thing happens on an arterial scan- caw

reason_raw: Tech Reviewed (B) Incorrect body part/laterality imaged
comment_raw:
Entire chest was scanned on this cta abdomen and pelvis

Hey! Per the TAVR protocol, the second scan is supposed to include the chest/abdomen/pelvis, a combination that was done several times today. The technologist did not make a mistake. This is the correct charge for this scan, because we can't double
charge for a CTA of the chest. Historically, the TAVR charge has included the charge for a CTA of the chest. Since we can't double charge, although we double scan, the charges for a TAVR CT work-up are CT Cardiac TAVR + CTA Abdomen Pelvis w/wo contrast-Chris

comment_raw:
No midline cines, only cine crossing the midline if the RLQ/LLQ, but there are no cines of the upper / mid mid abdomen.
Resident talked to tech and the reply was "Tanir said that the midline isn't part of their protocol so he didn't scan it this time"

This is important because if an invagination goes way up, it will start moving into the midline upper abdomen.

Copy paste from the peds protocols:

"CINE clips in all four quadrants, pelvis, and midline abdomen to document bowel"

Spoke with the new teammate and explained that no new images should be added to a completed exam unless directed by the Radiologist. I asked them to always confirm with the Radiologist if that is their preference regardless if the exam is ended and not
in Draft , Final or Pre-lim status.  Additionally if images are added to a study post dictation the rad must be notified immediately. The teammate thought that since the exam was just ended it was okay to add additional images.

Rad-
Initial CTA PE scan non-diagnostic due to contrast extravasation. Patient returned to ED, I told ED provider to reorder new CTA PE study if planning to reimage.

Original study dictated as non-diagnostic.

ED discussed with patient and sent her back to CT scanner. Techs re-scanned patient and added new PE images to this original study without notifying radiology.

Error caught because ED called and asked why original report says non-diagnostic when the new PE images are there. CT tech told ED no new image order needed when patient was brought down to be scanned

When I called tech to clarify, I was told on their end it showed exam ended so they decided to just add new images to existing study instead of asking for new image order. Nobody notified new images were added to existing study.

reason_raw: Tech Reviewed (M) Incorrect bolus timing
comment_raw:
This study was done in conjunction with a CTA TAVR- It's unfortunate that when ordered this way they are a hit or miss. The technologist also had this to share-

 I've used this protocol once before and it worked ok, but the combination of the timing of the C/A/P scan and the small quantity of contrast given seems like it could cause these scans to be more venous.  I talked to the radiologist who looked at this
TAVR and she was talking about the C/A/P being venous - which is scanned after the TAVR finishes.  The TAVR heart looks ok, there isn't a read on it at the time of writing this.  The protocol is set up in the scanner to use one bolus of 80mL of contrastand a 50mL saline chaser. The TAVR heart is scanned after triggering in the aorta. Then, with as minimal of a delay as possible the table moves to the lung apices and gives a breathing instruction for the Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis. The TAVR and the C/A/P arelinked and the move happens automatically.  The idea is to get an arterial C/A/P after the TAVR heart scan finishes with the same bolus.  I looked at the times for each part of the scan, and it was surprising to me. The monitoring phase for the TAVR
triggered in the aorta and finished scanning the heart 10 seconds later.  Then the table moved into position for the C/A/P and gave the breathing instruction.  At that point the C/A/P started 19 seconds after triggering in the aorta, and finished 4
seconds later.  For comparison, the dissection protocol, which is more like w...
